scope: Pass B full-surface expansion, re-verified directly against the live API (curl probes against https://store.zapier.com/api/records using a fresh, never-shared UUID4 secret; every test key created during probing was deleted afterward). The API has no published OpenAPI/swagger document, so live behavior is the source of truth over the help-center prose article. store.zapier.com is a single-resource key/value store with no list/search/pagination surface at all: exactly one path (/api/records) accepts 4 HTTP methods (GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE); PATCH multiplexes 7 further sub-operations through a required 'action' field in its JSON body (set_value_if, increment_by, delete, remove_child_value, set_child_value, list_push, list_pop), discovered by probing PATCH with an invalid body and reading the resulting JSON-schema validation error message it returns. This is the full extent of the real surface, not a scoping choice. GET returns a bare flat JSON object ({key: value, ...}) directly at the response root, NOT the {"records":[...]} array envelope this bundle's pre-existing streams.json/fixtures assume (a legacy-inherited wire-shape mismatch this pass documents but does not change; see docs.md's Known limits for the ENGINE_GAP).

excluded.reason: single-key lookup; live-verified to return a one-entry subset of the exact same flat object the records stream's unfiltered GET already reads, not a distinct resource shape

live-verified: POST with a JSON body merges/overwrites arbitrary caller-named top-level keys in one call (e.g. {"foo":"bar","baz":"qux"} sets both foo and baz); the dialect's JSON write body is built from a record's OWN static field names, so it cannot synthesize a body keyed by a runtime record VALUE (record.key='foo' -> body {"foo": ...}); set_record (PATCH action=set_value_if) covers the single-key-set use case this dialect CAN express instead

live-verified: PATCH action=delete removes a batch of keys given as a JSON array (data: ["k1","k2"]); the plain DELETE method with a ?key= query param (delete_record) already covers the single-key-delete use case this dialect's one-record-per-write-call model can express
removes one nested child key from a JSON-object-valued top-level key; a narrow nested-object mutation; Pass B breadth-vs-cost triage
sets one nested child key on a JSON-object-valued top-level key; a narrow nested-object mutation; Pass B breadth-vs-cost triage
appends to a list-valued top-level key; a narrow list-mutation shape distinct from plain key/value set; Pass B breadth-vs-cost triage
pops from a list-valued top-level key; a narrow list-mutation shape distinct from plain key/value set; Pass B breadth-vs-cost triage
